title: Control of turn-to-turn contact resistivity in resistively insulated REBCO coils
summary: Resistively insulated (RI) REBCO magnets feature short ramp times and low ramp losses while maintaining the advantages of no-insulation coils with high engineering current density and tolerance for defects in the REBCO conductor. Control of the turn-to-turn contact resistivity Rc is key to RI technology. Rc must be sufficiently high to prevent a large transient current, which could result in high mechanical stress during magnet quenches. Meanwhile it must be lower than the quench propagation limit to avoid conductor burn-out during a quench. Therefore, it is critical to control Rc within a suitable range of values which is usually coil specific. Previously, we discovered that Rc between two REBCO tapes with a stainless steel interlayer decreases dramatically with contact pressure cycling by up to three orders of magnitude. This drastic change made it impossible to design a suitable Rc value for a stainless steel co-wound RI magnet. In this work, we first present methods for mitigating Rc pressure cycling sensitivity. We found that by adding conductive fillers, such as conductive paste or epoxy, the Rc load cycling sensitivity is largely mitigated. For dry-wound coils, Rc load cycling sensitivity is mitigated by coating REBCO tape with a layer of 2- 3 um of PbSn solder. In addition, Rc can be controlled by oxidizing the stainless steel co-wind tape by heating stainless steel tapes at different temperatures in air. Using above methods, short sample tests showed that Rc was controlled to prescribed values of 1000 and 5000 uOhm-cm2 and was not sensitive to contact pressure cycling up to 30,000 cycles at 4.2 K. The new Rc control method was applied to a 6 double-pancake test coil which was tested at 4.2 K. The Rc in this test coil was comparable with the short sample results. This demonstrated the ability of this new method to control Rc in large coils.

AI.tldr: This work addresses a critical issue in resistively insulated (RI) REBCO magnets where turn-to-turn contact resistivity (Rc) becomes excessively sensitive to pressure cycling, making coil design unreliable. The authors propose three mitigation methods: adding conductive fillers, coating REBCO tape with PbSn solder, or oxidizing stainless steel co-wind tapes. These methods successfully stabilized Rc at prescribed values (1000-5000 μΩ-cm²) even after 30,000 pressure cycles at 4.2 K, and were validated on a 6 double-pancake test coil.
AI.motivation: The core motivation was to solve the problem of Rc pressure cycling sensitivity in RI REBCO magnets. Previously, Rc between REBCO tapes with stainless steel interlayer decreased dramatically (up to three orders of magnitude) with contact pressure cycling, making it impossible to design suitable Rc values for stainless steel co-wound RI magnets. This posed a serious risk: inadequate Rc could lead to either high mechanical stress during quenches (if too low) or conductor burn-out (if too high).
AI.method: Three methods were developed to mitigate Rc pressure cycling sensitivity: (1) Adding conductive fillers such as conductive paste or epoxy between contact surfaces; (2) For dry-wound coils, coating REBCO tape with a 2-3 μm layer of PbSn solder; (3) Oxidizing stainless steel co-wind tapes by heating at different temperatures in air. Short sample tests at 4.2 K verified Rc remained stable at prescribed values (1000 and 5000 μΩ-cm²) through up to 30,000 pressure cycles. The method was then applied to a 6 double-pancake test coil to demonstrate scalability.
AI.result: The three mitigation methods effectively reduced Rc load cycling sensitivity. Short sample tests showed Rc remained stable at prescribed values of 1000 and 5000 μΩ-cm² after 30,000 pressure cycles at 4.2 K. The 6 double-pancake test coil demonstrated comparable Rc values to short samples, confirming the method's effectiveness for large-scale coils.
AI.conclusion: The Rc pressure cycling sensitivity problem in RI REBCO magnets can be successfully mitigated through conductive fillers, PbSn solder coating, or stainless steel oxidation. These methods enable reliable Rc control within suitable ranges for magnet protection during quenches, making RI technology more viable for practical applications requiring high engineering current density, short ramp times, and low ramp losses.

title: Charge Density Wave Driven Topological Phase Transition in Vortices
summary: The interplay between charge density waves (CDWs) and superconductivity is a central theme in quantum materials, yet how CDW phase textures govern vortex topology remains poorly understood. We develop a theoretical framework showing that the phase of a stripe CDW can switch a magnetic vortex between topological and trivial regimes. Motivated by recent experiments, we propose two candidate mechanisms enabling phase-controlled switching of vortex topology. In a direct-modulation scenario, the CDW acts as a periodic potential that locally renormalizes band parameters and can induce topological transitions, but it generally cannot reproduce the symmetric node/antinode trend without fine tuning. In contrast, in an inversion-symmetry-breaking (ISB) scenario, a CDW node pinned to the vortex center breaks local inversion and allows for the mixture of spin-triplet pairing of Cooper pairs, producing a robust topological transition when this component dominates. Our results suggests CDW phase as a possible local handle to tune and test vortex topology.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically demonstrates that charge density wave (CDW) phase can control magnetic vortex topology in superconductors through two mechanisms: direct modulation and inversion-symmetry breaking, with the latter providing more robust topological transitions via spin-triplet pairing mixing.
AI.motivation: The interplay between charge density waves (CDWs) and superconductivity is a central theme in quantum materials, yet how CDW phase textures govern vortex topology remains poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical framework showing that the phase of a stripe CDW can switch a magnetic vortex between topological and trivial regimes. They propose two candidate mechanisms: (1) direct-modulation scenario where CDW acts as periodic potential renormalizing band parameters, and (2) inversion-symmetry-breaking (ISB) scenario where CDW node pinned to vortex center breaks local inversion, allowing spin-triplet pairing mixture.
AI.result: The direct-modulation scenario can induce topological transitions but generally cannot reproduce symmetric node/antinode trend without fine tuning. In contrast, the ISB scenario produces robust topological transition when spin-triplet component dominates.
AI.conclusion: CDW phase could serve as a possible local handle to tune and test vortex topology in quantum materials.

title: Ultrastrong Coupling Signatures in Photon Statistics from Terahertz Higgs-Polaritons
summary: The ultrastrong coupling regime of cavity photons and quantum materials has emerged as a pathway to modify materials properties, however definitive signatures of ultrastrong coupling remain elusive. Focusing on the quantum photon statistics of light transmitted through a cavity-embedded superconductor, we show that a two-photon Higgs polariton at strong coupling realizes a photonic nonlinearity at the single terahertz photon level. We find that as light-matter coupling increases, the photon statistics show pronounced changes due to the formation of a hybrid photon-matter dark-cavity state with finite photon occupancy, producing testable signatures of ultrastrong coupling. We derive a non-Markovian input output relation and study the cavity-embedded superconductor 2H-NbSe2 as it approaches ultrastrong light-matter coupling. Our results reveal a diagnostic for ultrastrong coupling in the two-photon coincidence statistics that is absent in total counts.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates ultrastrong coupling between cavity photons and superconducting materials, focusing on photon statistics as diagnostic signatures. Using 2H-NbSe2 as the material, the researchers find that two-photon coincidence statistics reveal pronounced changes due to hybrid photon-matter dark-cavity state formation, providing testable signatures absent in total photon counts.
AI.motivation: The ultrastrong coupling regime between cavity photons and quantum materials has emerged as a pathway to modify material properties, but definitive signatures of ultrastrong coupling remain elusive. The authors aim to address this gap by investigating quantum photon statistics.
AI.method: The researchers study photon statistics of light transmitted through a cavity-embedded superconductor, focusing on two-photon Higgs polariton at strong coupling. They develop a non-Markovian input-output relation and analyze cavity-embedded superconductor 2H-NbSe2 approaching ultrastrong light-matter coupling.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) Photon statistics show pronounced changes as light-matter coupling increases due to formation of hybrid photon-matter dark-cavity state with finite photon occupancy; (2) Two-photon coincidence statistics provide diagnostic for ultrastrong coupling that is absent in total counts; (3) A photonic nonlinearity at the single terahertz photon level is realized.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a diagnostic method for detecting ultrastrong coupling through two-photon coincidence statistics, offering testable signatures that were previously elusive. The findings have significant implications for understanding and utilizing ultrastrong light-matter coupling to modify material properties.

title: Laser induced surface nitriding of niobium: phase evolution and superconducting behaviour
summary: Laser nitriding represents a versatile approach for tailoring the surface properties of metals. Up to now, its effect on the superconducting response of niobium nitrides remains largely unexplored. In this work, the nitriding process of niobium by laser irradiation under a controlled nitrogen atmosphere up to 2.50 bar, using a nanosecond pulsed laser with wavelength of 1064 nm has been investigated. By independently tuning the nitrogen pressure, the two-dimensional accumulated fluence ($F_{2D}$) and the laser irradiance, a laser-processing map for the formation of either a combination of $β$-Nb$_2$N (hexagonal) and $γ$-Nb$_4$N$_{3\pm x}$ (tetragonal) phases or only the $β$-phase has been established. Systematic analysis by X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy and electron backscatter diffraction revealed that the nitrogen-rich $γ$-phase forms in the near-surface layer through melting when $F_{2D}$ exceeds a certain value ($> 50 \,\mathrm{kJ/cm^2}$ at 2.50 bar). A $β$-layer is observed underneath, and further inside, there is a band of embedded $β$-grains in the Nb matrix. Their size gradually decreases with increasing distance to surface, suggesting thermal gradients and a diffusion formation mechanism. When the $γ$-phase becomes predominant, a significant increase in the superconducting critical temperature is observed, up to $T_c \approx 15\,\mathrm{K}$, and magnetic irreversibility. For low $F_{2D}$ values ($\approx 7.5 \,\mathrm{kJ/cm^2}$ at 1.50-2.50 bar), the formation of a uniform nitride layer composed of sub-micron-sized $β$-Nb$_2$N grains results in a ca. fourfold enhancement in surface microhardness. These findings provide fundamental insights into laser-induced nitriding of niobium to engineer mechanically robust and superconducting Nb-N layers.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates laser nitriding of niobium under controlled nitrogen atmosphere (up to 2.50 bar) using a nanosecond pulsed laser (1064 nm). By independently tuning nitrogen pressure, 2D accumulated fluence (F2D), and laser irradiance, a processing map was established for forming specific niobium nitride phases. When F2D exceeds 50 kJ/cm2 at 2.50 bar, the nitrogen-rich γ-phase forms in near-surface layers through melting, achieving Tc ≈ 15 K and magnetic irreversibility. At low F2D (~7.5 kJ/cm2), a uniform β-phase layer with sub-micron grains provides ~4x enhancement in surface microhardness.
AI.motivation: Laser nitriding is a versatile approach for tailoring surface properties of metals, but its effect on the superconducting response of niobium nitrides remained largely unexplored. This work aimed to fill this knowledge gap by systematically investigating the laser-induced nitriding process to engineer both mechanically robust and superconducting Nb-N layers.
AI.method: Nanosecond pulsed laser (1064 nm wavelength) was used to nitride niobium under controlled nitrogen atmosphere up to 2.50 bar. Three key parameters were independently tuned: nitrogen pressure, two-dimensional accumulated fluence (F2D), and laser irradiance. Samples were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD).
AI.result: A laser-processing map was established for formation of either β-Nb2N (hexagonal) + γ-Nb4N3±x (tetragonal) phases or only β-phase. For F2D > 50 kJ/cm2 at 2.50 bar: γ-phase forms in near-surface layer through melting, with underlying β-layer and embedded β-grains in Nb matrix (grain size decreasing with depth). Predominant γ-phase gives Tc ≈ 15 K and magnetic irreversibility. For low F2D (~7.5 kJ/cm2 at 1.50-2.50 bar): uniform nitride layer of sub-micron β-Nb2N grains with ~4x enhancement in surface microhardness.
AI.conclusion: Laser nitriding under controlled nitrogen atmosphere enables precise engineering of Nb-N surface layers with tunable structure and properties. High fluence processing yields superconducting γ-phase (Tc ~15 K) while low fluence produces mechanically hardened β-phase layers. These findings provide fundamental insights for tailoring niobium surface properties for applications requiring combined mechanical robustness and superconductivity.

title: Exascale Multi-Task Graph Foundation Models for Imbalanced, Multi-Fidelity Atomistic Data
summary: We present an exascale workflow for materials discovery using atomistic graph foundation models built on HydraGNN. We jointly train on 16 open first-principles datasets (544+ million structures covering 85+ elements) using a multi-task architecture with per-dataset heads and a scalable ADIOS2/DDStore data pipeline. On Frontier, we execute six large-scale DeepHyper hyperparameter optimization campaigns in FP64 and promote the top-performing message-passing models to sustained 2,048-node training, yielding a PaiNN-based lead model. The resulting model enables billion-scale screening, evaluating 1.1 billion atomistic structures in 50 seconds, compressing a workload that would require years of first-principles computation, and supports data-scarce fine-tuning across diverse downstream tasks. We quantify precision-performance tradeoffs (BF16/FP32/FP64), demonstrate transfer across twelve chemically diverse downstream tasks, and establish seamless strong- and weak-scaling across Frontier, Aurora, and Perlmutter. This work allows fast and reliable exploration of vast chemical design spaces that are otherwise inaccessible to first-principles methods.

AI.tldr: An exascale workflow for materials discovery using atomistic graph foundation models (HydraGNN) trained on 16 first-principles datasets (544M+ structures, 85+ elements), enabling billion-scale screening of 1.1B structures in 50 seconds.
AI.motivation: The need for fast and reliable exploration of vast chemical design spaces that are otherwise inaccessible to first-principles methods, which require years of computation.
AI.method: Joint multi-task training on 16 datasets with per-dataset heads, scalable ADIOS2/DDStore data pipeline, DeepHyper hyperparameter optimization in FP64, 2,048-node training on Frontier, PaiNN-based architecture, precision tradeoffs (BF16/FP32/FP64).
AI.result: Billion-scale screening capability: 1.1B atomistic structures evaluated in 50 seconds (vs. years of first-principles computation). Transfer learning across 12 downstream tasks. Strong/weak scaling on Frontier, Aurora, and Perlmutter.
AI.conclusion: This work enables fast and reliable exploration of vast chemical design spaces that were previously inaccessible to first-principles methods, with support for data-scarce fine-tuning across diverse downstream applications.

title: Fluctuating Pair Density Wave in Finite-temperature Phase Diagram of the $t$-$t^\prime$ Hubbard Model
summary: The Hubbard model and its extensions are canonical theoretical frameworks for understanding correlated electronic states, including those in high-$T_c$ cuprates. Here, we use state-of-the-art thermal tensor network method to map out the temperature-doping phase diagram of the $t$-$t^\prime$ Hubbard model. On the electron-doped side, we find a $d$-wave superconducting (dSC) regime, supporting the scenario of high-$T_c$ superconductivity. In contrast, on the hole-doped side, no robust dSC phase is detected. Instead, a finite-temperature regime dominated by strong pair-density-wave (PDW) fluctuations emerges, which may eventually give way to charge density wave order upon further cooling. The PDW state exhibits inter-arc pairing with net momentum near $(0, π)$, distinct from the zero-momentum pairing in conventional dSC. Furthermore, these fluctuating PDW states occupy the lower portion of the pseudogap regime on the hole-doped side. We provide a comprehensive finite-temperature perspective consistent with previous ground-state studies, shedding new light on pairing instabilities and exotic electronic states in high-$T_c$ superconductors.

AI.tldr: This study uses thermal tensor network methods to map the temperature-doping phase diagram of the t-t' Hubbard model, finding d-wave superconductivity on the electron-doped side but only pair-density-wave fluctuations (not robust superconductivity) on the hole-doped side, with PDW states occupying the lower pseudogap regime.
AI.motivation: The Hubbard model is a canonical framework for understanding high-Tc cuprates, but finite-temperature behavior and the mechanism of superconductivity remain controversial. This work aims to provide a comprehensive finite-temperature perspective on pairing instabilities.
AI.method: State-of-the-art thermal tensor network method to study the temperature-doping phase diagram of the t-t' Hubbard model.
AI.result: On electron-doped side: robust d-wave superconducting phase exists. On hole-doped side: no robust dSC found; instead strong PDW fluctuations emerge at finite temperatures, possibly giving way to charge density wave order upon cooling. PDW shows inter-arc pairing with net momentum near (0, π), distinct from conventional zero-momentum pairing.
AI.conclusion: The asymmetry between electron-doped and hole-doped sides supports different pairing scenarios - conventional dSC for electrons but exotic PDW fluctuations for holes. These fluctuating PDW states occupy the lower pseudogap regime, providing new insight into pairing instabilities in high-Tc superconductors.

title: Inelastic neutron scattering study on the AFM uniform spin-1/2 chain compound CuSb2O6
summary: We carried out inelastic neutron scattering experiments on a powdered sample of the antiferromagnetic (AFM) uniform spin-1/2 chain compound CuSb2O6.The magnetic excitations appear in the energy range of 1.8 to 13 meV at 2.5 K below the AFM transition temperature (TN = 8.7 K).The gap value (1.8 meV) is close to that evaluated from the specific heat (1.51 meV). The excitations at 12.5 K (> TN) appear gapless. Thus, the 1.8 meV gap is caused by some anisotropy in spin-wave excitations. The gap excitations are strongest at 0.48 corresponding to a length of 0.66 nm. This result is consistent with the theoretical one that the interaction in a Cu pair with a length of 0.65562 nm (Jab) is strongest. The magnetic excitations can be explained by the AFM uniform XXZ chain with Jab = 6.437 meV and DJab = 0.063 meV. The 1.8 meV gap is caused by the small Ising anisotropy (DJab/Jab = 0.0098).

AI.tldr: Inelastic neutron scattering experiments on CuSb2O6 antiferromagnetic spin-1/2 chain compound reveal a gap of 1.8 meV at 2.5 K, explained by Ising anisotropy in the AFM uniform XXZ chain model.
AI.motivation: Understanding the magnetic excitation properties and anisotropy effects in the antiferromagnetic uniform spin-1/2 chain compound CuSb2O6, particularly the origin of the energy gap observed below the Néel temperature.
AI.method: Inelastic neutron scattering experiments on powdered CuSb2O6 sample at temperatures of 2.5 K and 12.5 K (below and above TN = 8.7 K), measuring magnetic excitations in the energy range of 1.8 to 13 meV.
AI.result: The magnetic excitations show a gap of 1.8 meV at 2.5 K (below TN), becoming gapless above TN. The gap value matches specific heat measurements (1.51 meV). The excitations are strongest at Q = 0.48 Å^-1 (0.66 nm), consistent with theoretical Cu pair interaction length of 0.65562 nm. Data fits AFM uniform XXZ chain model with Jab = 6.437 meV and ΔJab = 0.063 meV, yielding small Ising anisotropy of DJab/Jab = 0.0098.
AI.conclusion: The 1.8 meV gap in CuSb2O6 originates from small Ising anisotropy in spin-wave excitations, not from a spin Peierls transition, and can be well described by the AFM uniform XXZ chain model with anisotropic parameter ΔJab/Jab = 0.0098.

title: Atomic-scale order enables high thermal boundary conductance at $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$/4H-SiC interfaces
summary: Thermal boundary conductance (TBC) at dissimilar interfaces imposes a fundamental limit on electronic device performance, yet predicting and understanding heat transport across realistic, disordered boundaries remains elusive. Here, we develop a computational framework that combines machine-learned interatomic potentials with lattice dynamics to address the long-standing problem of how interfacial structure, from disordered to atomically sharp, affects thermal transport in the technologically important $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$/4H-SiC heterostructure. By explicitly accounting for phonon wave-particle duality, we show that interfacial disorder introduces additional interfacial phonon modes that facilitate vibrational impedance matching between the two highly dissimilar crystals, yet it simultaneously disrupts interfacial phonon coherence and limits the potential heat-transport benefit. Our atomistic simulations further indicate that restoring atomic-scale order preserves coherence and yields markedly higher conductance. These insights motivate the controlled epitaxial growth of $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$/4H-SiC heterostructures with systematically tuned interfacial order. Experimental measurements validate our predictions, achieving a record-high TBC of 231 MW m$^{-2}$ K$^{-1}$ at atomically sharp junctions. Beyond the immediate implications for $β$-Ga$_2$O$_3$-based power electronics, our results establish the preservation of interfacial phonon coherence as an effective strategy for mitigating thermal bottlenecks in mismatched systems.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses thermal boundary conductance at β-Ga₂O₃/4H-SiC interfaces using machine learning and lattice dynamics. It discovers that while disorder helps impedance matching, it disrupts phonon coherence; atomically sharp interfaces achieve record 231 MW m⁻² K⁻¹ conductance by preserving phonon coherence.
AI.motivation: The fundamental problem is predicting and understanding heat transport across realistic, disordered boundaries in electronic devices. Thermal boundary conductance (TBC) at dissimilar interfaces limits electronic device performance, but understanding how interfacial structure affects thermal transport has remained elusive.
AI.method: The researchers developed a computational framework combining machine-learned interatomic potentials with lattice dynamics to study β-Ga₂O₃/4H-SiC heterostructures. They explicitly accounted for phonon wave-particle duality to analyze how different interfacial structures (from disordered to atomically sharp) affect thermal transport.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Interfacial disorder introduces additional interfacial phonon modes that facilitate vibrational impedance matching between dissimilar crystals; (2) However, disorder simultaneously disrupts interfacial phonon coherence and limits heat-transport benefits; (3) Restoring atomic-scale order preserves coherence and yields markedly higher conductance. Experimental validation showed record-high TBC of 231 MW m⁻² K⁻¹ at atomically sharp junctions.
AI.conclusion: Preserving interfacial phonon coherence is an effective strategy for mitigating thermal bottlenecks in mismatched systems. This motivates controlled epitaxial growth of β-Ga₂O₃/4H-SiC heterostructures with systematically tuned interfacial order for power electronics applications.

title: Inductance Meets Memory in the Quantum Magnet Mn3Si2Te6
summary: Orbital degrees of freedom offer a largely untapped route to emergent dynamical phenomena in correlated quantum materials. However, it remains unclear whether collective orbital states can intrinsically generate both reactive and memory functionalities in a bulk system. Here we show that in the ferrimagnet Mn3Si2Te6, nonequilibrium reconfiguration of chiral orbital currents produces both emergent inductance and nonvolatile memristance as intrinsic properties of a single crystal. At low frequency and under a magnetic field along the c axis, coherent orbital-current domains generate robust clockwise inductive I-V loops. At higher frequency and low field, current-driven first-order reconfiguration leads to incomplete reversal and metastable trapping, producing an intrinsic electromotive force and a finite remanent voltage at zero current. These results establish orbital currents as a class of quantum state variables that encode both reactive and memory functionalities, opening routes toward intrinsically reconfigurable and energy-efficient electronic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that the ferrimagnet Mn3Si2Te6 exhibits both emergent inductance and nonvolatile memristance through nonequilibrium reconfiguration of chiral orbital currents, establishing orbital currents as intrinsic quantum state variables encoding both reactive and memory functionalities.
AI.motivation: Orbital degrees of freedom remain an untapped route to emergent dynamical phenomena in correlated quantum materials. It was unclear whether collective orbital states can intrinsically generate both reactive and memory functionalities in a bulk system.
AI.method: The researchers investigated the ferrimagnet Mn3Si2Te6 by applying magnetic fields along the c-axis and measuring I-V characteristics at different frequencies to observe coherent orbital-current domain behavior and current-driven first-order reconfiguration.
AI.result: At low frequency with magnetic field along c-axis, coherent orbital-current domains generate robust clockwise inductive I-V loops. At higher frequency and low field, current-driven first-order reconfiguration leads to incomplete reversal and metastable trapping, producing an intrinsic electromotive force and finite remanent voltage at zero current.
AI.conclusion: Orbital currents represent a class of quantum state variables that encode both reactive and memory functionalities, opening new routes toward intrinsically reconfigurable and energy-efficient electronic systems.

title: Device-area selection of memristive transport regimes in epitaxial $Hf_{0.5}Zr_{0.5}O_{2}$-based ferroelectric devices
summary: Ferroelectric memristive devices based on hafnia are promising systems for neuromorphic electronics, yet the interplay between polarization-modulated resistive changes and defect-mediated transport often leads to complex and debated switching mechanisms. Here, we investigate this competition in epitaxial Hf$_{0.5}$Zr$_{0.5}$O$_2$/La$_{0.67}$Sr$_{0.33}$MnO$_3$ heterostructures with Pt top electrodes by combining structural, ferroelectric, and memristive characterization with a statistical analysis across a broad range of device areas spanning three orders of magnitude. We identify two distinct memristive regimes with opposite resistance--voltage chiralities. Small devices exhibit a low-resistance state that scales inversely with area, consistent with area-distributed tunneling transport, while larger devices display an area-independent resistance indicative of localized conductive channels. A statistical nucleation model quantitatively captures this behavior and yields a crossover characteristic area $A^* \approx 10^3~μ\mathrm{m}^2$. This crossover also correlates with the onset of ferroelectric wake-up in larger devices, linking conductive-channel nucleation and oxygen-vacancy redistribution within a unified physical picture. These results establish lateral device size as a key parameter controlling the dominant transport mechanism in epitaxial hafnia-based devices.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the switching mechanisms in epitaxial Hf0.5Zr0.5O2/La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 ferroelectric memristive devices by combining structural/ferroelectric/memristive characterization with statistical analysis across devices spanning three orders of magnitude in area. Two distinct memristive regimes with opposite resistance-voltage chiralities were identified: small devices show area-distributed tunneling transport, while larger devices exhibit localized conductive channels. A statistical nucleation model yields a crossover area A* ≈ 10^3 μm^2, which correlates with ferroelectric wake-up, establishing device lateral size as a key parameter controlling transport mechanism.
AI.motivation: The interplay between polarization-modulated resistive changes and defect-mediated transport in ferroelectric hafnia devices leads to complex and debated switching mechanisms. Understanding this competition is crucial for developing reliable neuromorphic electronics.
AI.method: The researchers combined structural, ferroelectric, and memristive characterization with statistical analysis across devices spanning three orders of magnitude in area (from small to large lateral dimensions). They used epitaxial Hf0.5Zr0.5O2/La0.67Sr0.33MnO3 heterostructures with Pt top electrodes.
AI.result: Two distinct memristive regimes with opposite resistance-voltage chiralities were identified. Small devices exhibit low-resistance states scaling inversely with area (consistent with area-distributed tunneling transport), while larger devices show area-independent resistance (indicative of localized conductive channels). The statistical nucleation model yields a crossover characteristic area A* ≈ 10^3 μm^2, which correlates with the onset of ferroelectric wake-up in larger devices.
AI.conclusion: Lateral device size is established as a key parameter controlling the dominant transport mechanism in epitaxial hafnia-based devices. The study links conductive-channel nucleation and oxygen-vacancy redistribution within a unified physical picture, providing important insights for designing reliable ferroelectric memristive devices for neuromorphic applications.

title: Enhancing Neural-Network Variational Monte Carlo through Basis Transformation
summary: Neural-network variational Monte Carlo (NNVMC) has emerged as a powerful tool for solving quantum many-body problems, yet systematic pathways for improving its accuracy remain largely heuristic. Here, we introduce a physically motivated basis transformation for NNVMC that enhances variational expressivity without increasing the complexity of the neural-network ansatz itself. By formulating the many-body wave function in a Gaussian basis, we introduce a single learnable locality parameter, $α$, that reshapes the target ground state into a more learnable representation. This approach introduces minimal computational overhead and can be readily combined with existing neural-network architectures. Using the three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas as a benchmark, we show that the optimized basis transformation consistently lowers the variational energy for both FermiNet and message-passing neural-network architectures. Notably, for the latter, it enables a more precise determination of the Fermi liquid to Wigner crystal phase transition. More broadly, our results highlight basis transformation as a new route to improving NNVMC in continuous space, showing that accuracy can be enhanced not only by refining the ansatz but also by making the target ground state easier to represent.

AI.tldr: A Gaussian basis transformation with a learnable locality parameter α is introduced to enhance variational expressivity in neural-network variational Monte Carlo (NNVMC) for quantum many-body problems, without increasing neural-network complexity.
AI.motivation: NNVMC is powerful for quantum many-body problems but lacks systematic improvement pathways beyond heuristic ansatz refinement. The authors seek a physically motivated way to enhance variational expressivity.
AI.method: The many-body wave function is reformulated in a Gaussian basis, introducing a single learnable locality parameter α that reshapes the target ground state into a more learnable representation. This adds minimal computational overhead and can be combined with existing architectures.
AI.result: Applied to 3D homogeneous electron gas: consistently lowers variational energy for both FermiNet and message-passing NN architectures. Enables more precise determination of Fermi liquid to Wigner crystal phase transition for the latter.
AI.conclusion: Basis transformation is a new route to improving NNVMC in continuous space - accuracy can be enhanced not only by refining the ansatz but also by making the target ground state easier to represent.

title: Electronic Signature of Melting Onset in Polycrystalline Copper at Extreme Conditions
summary: Ultrafast melting is fundamentally a structural transition of the ionic lattice, but this rearrangement also reshapes the electronic properties by changing the energy landscape and scattering mechanisms. Although the electrons react almost instantaneously, it is not {\it a priori} clear how much lattice disorder is required for a significant response. Here, we show that the onset of melting already produces a clear electronic signature in polycrystalline copper. Using single-shot terahertz time-domain spectroscopy on thin films excited over a wide range of laser fluences, we infer the transient conductivity during the first picoseconds after excitation. The data, supported by two-temperature molecular-dynamics simulations, show that before melting, electron transport is substantially limited by grain-boundary scattering and that melting strongly suppresses this channel. As melting begins at these interfaces, we observe a transient increase in the conductivity that directly marks the onset of the phase transition. More broadly, these results show that ionic and electronic relaxation stages are closely coupled in nonequilibrium laser-driven matter and that optical measurements can resolve distinct stages of melting.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how ultrafast melting affects electronic properties in polycrystalline copper, showing that grain-boundary scattering significantly limits electron transport before melting, and that the onset of melting produces a detectable electronic signature via terahertz time-domain spectroscopy.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to understand the coupling between ionic structural changes and electronic properties during ultrafast melting, specifically how much lattice disorder is needed to produce a significant electronic response and whether the onset of melting can be detected through electronic measurements.
AI.method: The researchers used single-shot terahertz time-domain spectroscopy on thin films of polycrystalline copper excited over a wide range of laser fluences, measuring transient conductivity during the first picoseconds after excitation. They supported the experimental data with two-temperature molecular-dynamics simulations.
AI.result: They found that before melting, electron transport is substantially limited by grain-boundary scattering. As melting begins at grain interfaces, this scattering channel is strongly suppressed, leading to a transient increase in conductivity that directly marks the onset of the phase transition.
AI.conclusion: The conclusions are that ionic and electronic relaxation stages are closely coupled in nonequilibrium laser-driven matter, and that optical measurements like terahertz spectroscopy can resolve distinct stages of the melting process, providing a clear electronic signature for detecting the onset of melting.

title: Experimental quantification of electronic symmetry breaking through orbital hybridization phase
summary: Symmetry classification of crystal structures has been central to predicting physical properties of materials. While such structural classification identifies which physical responses are symmetry-allowed, the magnitudes of these responses are governed by the degree of symmetry breaking in the electronic state. However, a well-defined quantitative descriptor for the electronic symmetry breaking has been established only in limited cases such as electric polarization and magnetization. No analogous descriptor exists for most other types, including chirality. Here, we propose an experimental framework for quantifying electronic symmetry breaking from the anisotropy of valence electron density distribution. We show that the orbital hybridization phases governing this anisotropy can be uniquely determined under site symmetry constraints. Applying this framework to structurally chiral transition-metal silicides, we determine hybridization phases from their valence electron densities observed by synchrotron X-ray diffraction. From the obtained complex hybridization, we quantify an electronic chirality $χ$ and theoretically demonstrate that it is directly proportional to circular dichroism, establishing $χ$ as a predictive descriptor of chiral responses. This approach is systematically applicable to various point groups, offering a general route to quantifying electronic symmetry breaking and predicting associated physical properties.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an experimental framework to quantify electronic symmetry breaking by analyzing valence electron density anisotropy via synchrotron X-ray diffraction. The method determines orbital hybridization phases under site symmetry constraints and quantifies electronic chirality χ, establishing its direct proportionality to circular dichroism for predicting chiral responses across various point groups.
AI.motivation: While symmetry classification identifies allowed physical responses, there was no quantitative descriptor for electronic symmetry breaking magnitude (e.g., chirality), limiting predictive capability of chiral responses.
AI.method: The authors propose an experimental framework using synchrotron X-ray diffraction to observe valence electron densities, determining orbital hybridization phases under site symmetry constraints to quantify electronic symmetry breaking.
AI.result: Applied to chiral transition-metal silicides, the framework determines hybridization phases from valence electron densities, enabling quantification of electronic chirality χ that is directly proportional to circular dichroism.
AI.conclusion: This framework provides a general method for quantifying electronic chirality χ, establishing its direct proportionality to circular dichroism and enabling prediction of chiral physical responses.

title: Hole concentrations in doped gray α-Sn on InSb and CdTe measured with infrared ellipsometry
summary: Gray tin (α-Sn) layers with 30 nm thickness were grown on InSb (001) substrates using molecular beam epitaxy. The surface preparation of the substrates was adjusted to achieve either n-type or p-type doping in the α-Sn layer. Fourier-transform infrared ellipsometry was used to find the temperature-dependent dielectric function of the α-Sn layers from 0.03 to 0.8 eV and from 10 to 300 K. Because of the inverted band structure of α-Sn, the spectra show a strong absorption peak at 0.45 eV due to transitions from the inverted {Γ_-^7} "electron" valence band to the {Γ_+^8} heavy hole valence band. Applying the Thomas-Reiche-Kuhn f-sum rule, the integrated oscillator strength of this peak was used to calculate the heavy hole concentration as a function of temperature. For a nearly intrinsic α-Sn layer, the heavy hole concentration agrees well with predictions based on degenerate Fermi-Dirac statistics. Deviations from the intrinsic α-Sn carrier concentrations are attributed to substrate surface preparation leading to the diffusion of donor or acceptor ions into the α-Sn layer causing n-type or p-type doping.

AI.tldr: This paper studies gray tin (α-Sn) thin layers grown on InSb substrates using molecular beam epitaxy, investigating their temperature-dependent dielectric properties and carrier concentrations through infrared ellipsometry.
AI.motivation: Gray tin (α-Sn) has an unusual inverted band structure that creates unique optical transitions. The research aimed to understand how substrate preparation affects doping type and to accurately measure heavy hole concentrations using the f-sum rule.
AI.method: Molecular beam epitaxy was used to grow 30 nm α-Sn layers on InSb (001) substrates with varying surface preparation to achieve n-type or p-type doping. Fourier-transform infrared ellipsometry measured the dielectric function from 0.03-0.8 eV and 10-300 K. The Thomas-Reiche-Kuhn f-sum rule was applied to the 0.45 eV absorption peak to calculate heavy hole concentration.
AI.result: A strong absorption peak at 0.45 eV was observed, arising from transitions between the inverted Γ₇⁻ valence band and the Γ₈⁺ heavy hole valence band. For nearly intrinsic samples, heavy hole concentrations agreed well with degenerate Fermi-Dirac statistics. Deviations from intrinsic behavior were attributed to dopant diffusion from substrate surface preparation.
AI.conclusion: Substrate surface preparation significantly influences doping in α-Sn layers through donor/acceptor diffusion. The f-sum rule provides an effective method for determining carrier concentrations in these materials with inverted band structures.

title: Ultrafast Current Switching from Quantum Geometry in Semimetals
summary: Technological progress towards next-generation electronics critically relies on achieving faster switching with reduced energy consumption. Because device operation speeds are fundamentally constrained by the intrinsic properties of constituent materials, identifying systems with inherently superior switching capabilities is essential. Here, we propose that semimetallic systems characterized by non-trivial quantum geometry, including quadratic band-touching semimetals and singular flat bands, can serve as a promising platform for ultrafast switching at voltages compatible with modern electronics. We show that, in such quantum geometric semimetals, an electric current is generated instantaneously upon application of a moderate external electric field, reaching its steady-state value. As a consequence, the current exhibits rapid and stable on-off switching behaviour under periodic optical pulse trains, demonstrating robustness under experimentally feasible conditions. In terms of switching speed, this quantum geometric semimetal outperforms conventional metals, semiconductors, and graphene. We identify the microscopic origin of this behaviour as interband coupling governed by the Hilbert-Schmidt quantum distance, together with a finite density of states at the band-touching point. This mechanism further leads to a universal classification of conductivity for both gapless and gapped quantum geometric semimetals. Finally, first-principles calculations suggest realistic material platforms, including bilayer graphene, cyclic graphene, monolayer bismuth and V3F8-in which the predicted instantaneous current switching can be directly realized, further supported by time-dependent density functional theory simulations performed for representative systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using quantum geometric semimetals (with quadratic band-touching or singular flat bands) for ultrafast, low-energy electronics. Current in these materials responds instantaneously to electric fields, enabling fast on-off switching that outperforms conventional materials. The mechanism relies on interband coupling governed by quantum geometry, with realistic material candidates identified through first-principles calculations.
AI.motivation: Next-generation electronics require faster switching with reduced energy consumption. Current device speeds are limited by intrinsic material properties, necessitating identification of materials with superior switching capabilities.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using Hilbert-Schmidt quantum distance to characterize interband coupling, combined with first-principles calculations and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) simulations.
AI.result: In quantum geometric semimetals, electric current generates instantaneously upon applying moderate electric field, reaching steady-state value. This enables rapid, stable on-off switching under periodic optical pulse trains. QGS outperforms conventional metals, semiconductors, and graphene in switching speed. A universal conductivity classification for both gapless and gapped QGS was established.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes quantum geometric semimetals (QGS) as a promising platform for ultrafast switching devices, outperforming conventional materials. The mechanism based on Hilbert-Schmidt quantum distance and finite DOS provides a universal classification framework. First-principles calculations and TDDFT simulations confirm feasibility in realistic materials including bilayer graphene, cyclic graphene, monolayer bismuth, and V3F8.

title: Facet-dependent Chemical Kinetics Governed Growth of Twisted Graphene Layers with Pre-designed Angles
comment: 43 pages, 6 figures. Main text only, including Methods and References
summary: Twisted graphene layers (TGLs) provide a powerful platform for investigating multiple quantum phenomena, yet their scalable deployment is hindered by the lack of reliable synthesis with precise angle. Here, benefited from a deeper understanding of the interplay between grain index and graphene growth kinetics, we report a scalable strategy to grow TGLs with pre-designed twist angles on platinum (Pt) via chemical vapor deposition (CVD), Through a combination of complementary in situ methods, we identified the activity sequence of different Pt grains and attributed it to the area ratio of exposed (110) facets during graphene-induced surface reconstruction. Moreover, we revealed that CVD-grown graphene orientation is determined by the grain-orientation-dependent surface morphology. By leveraging the so-established correlations between grain index with both graphene growth priority and its orientation, we achieve controlled folding and tearing of graphene overlayer using a pair of adjacent grains with dramatically different catalytical activity and kink-free atomic steps. We reveal that overlayer-induced step bunching and terrace reconfiguration critically govern the domain morphology and folding direction. Building on this mechanistic insight, we demonstrate a substrate-engineering framework where specific platinum grains are rationally selected to yield TGLs with pre-designed twist angles, including magic angle with flat band dispersion. This work not only highlights fundamental kinetics of Pt catalyzed graphene CVD growth, but also offers a generalizable methodology for manipulating foldable two-dimensional materials via dynamic substrate reconstruction, exampled by programmable growth of high-quality TGLs on open surfaces.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a scalable CVD strategy to grow twisted graphene layers (TGLs) with pre-designed twist angles on platinum substrates by exploiting grain-orientation-dependent catalytic activity and surface reconstruction.
AI.motivation: The lack of reliable synthesis methods with precise twist angles hinders the scalable deployment of twisted graphene layers for investigating quantum phenomena (like magic angle superconductivity).
AI.method: The researchers used complementary in situ methods to identify the activity sequence of different Pt grains, linking it to the area ratio of exposed (110) facets during graphene-induced surface reconstruction. They correlated grain index with graphene growth priority and orientation, then used adjacent grains with different catalytic activity and kink-free atomic steps to control overlayer folding and tearing.
AI.result: They established a substrate-engineering framework that allows rational selection of specific Pt grains to produce TGLs with pre-designed twist angles, including the magic angle with flat band dispersion.
AI.conclusion: This work provides fundamental insights into Pt-catalyzed graphene CVD growth kinetics and offers a generalizable methodology for manipulating foldable 2D materials via dynamic substrate reconstruction, enabling programmable growth of high-quality TGLs on open surfaces.

title: Antiferromagnetic Dimers in the Parent Phase of a Correlated Kagome Superconductor
summary: Kagome metals are prone to charge-density wave (CDW), magnetic, and superconducting phases, with their flat electronic band conducive for correlated physics. In contrast to the weakly correlated $A$V$_3$Sb$_5$ ($A$ = K, Rb, Cs) kagome metals with a $2\times2$ CDW, CsCr$_3$Sb$_5$ is a correlated metal with a flat band close to the Fermi level, and exhibits a $4\times1$ CDW intertwined with magnetic order. Under pressure, the intertwined orders are suppressed and give way to a dome of superconductivity that emerges from a non-Fermi liquid normal state. Here, we solve the crystal structure of the $4\times 1$ CDW state in CsCr$_3$Sb$_5$, and show it consists of Cr dimers separated by Cr chains. First-principles calculations show the dominant exchange interaction is antiferromagnetic within the dimers, while the intra-chain and dimer-chain couplings are much weaker. The CDW transition of CsCr$_3$Sb$_5$ is found to be more strongly first-order than those in $A$V$_3$Sb$_5$, without significant soft phonons or diffuse scattering above the CDW transition temperature. These findings suggest that fluctuating antiferromagnetic dimers may play a major role in the electron pairing of superconducting CsCr$_3$Sb$_5$.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the 4×1 charge-density wave (CDW) state in CsCr₃Sb₅, a correlated kagome metal with a flat band near the Fermi level. The crystal structure of the CDW state was solved, revealing Cr dimers separated by Cr chains. First-principles calculations show dominant antiferromagnetic exchange within dimers, with weaker intra-chain and dimer-chain couplings. The CDW transition is more strongly first-order than in AV₃Sb₅ compounds, lacking soft phonons or diffuse scattering above the transition temperature. The findings suggest fluctuating antiferromagnetic dimers may contribute to electron pairing in the superconducting state that emerges under pressure.
AI.motivation: CsCr₃Sb₅ exhibits a unique 4×1 CDW intertwined with magnetic order, in contrast to the weakly correlated AV₃Sb₅ (A = K, Rb, Cs) kagome metals with 2×2 CDW. Under pressure, the intertwined orders are suppressed, giving way to a dome of superconductivity emerging from a non-Fermi liquid normal state, making this system important for understanding correlated physics in kagome metals.
AI.method: The crystal structure of the 4×1 CDW state in CsCr₃Sb₅ was solved using structural analysis. First-principles calculations were performed to determine the exchange interactions, specifically comparing antiferromagnetic within dimers versus intra-chain and dimer-chain couplings.
AI.result: The 4×1 CDW state consists of Cr dimers separated by Cr chains. The dominant exchange interaction is antiferromagnetic within the dimers, while intra-chain and dimer-chain couplings are much weaker. The CDW transition is more strongly first-order than in AV₃Sb₅, without significant soft phonons or diffuse scattering above the CDW transition temperature.
AI.conclusion: Fluctuating antiferromagnetic dimers may play a major role in the electron pairing of superconducting CsCr₃Sb₅. This provides insight into the mechanism of superconductivity in correlated kagome metals where flat bands and intertwined orders are present.

title: Fully compensated and uncompensated ferrimagnetic ferrovalley semiconductors
summary: Altermagnets (AMs) and fully compensated ferrimagnets (FC-FIMs) are emerging classes of magnetic materials that combine the advantages of antiferromagnets and ferromagnets. Here, we elucidate the mechanism behind the uniaxial strain-driven transformation from AM to FC-FIM and find that the accompanying non-relativistic valley polarization is positively correlated with the net magnetic moment between magnetic atoms in opposite spin sublattices. We then propose an uncompensated ferrimagnetic monolayer VCrSeTeO to achieve large intrinsic valley polarization. Spin-orbit coupling (SOC) is shown to further increase the valley polarization to over 400 meV under uniaxial strains and the reason is explained in terms of SOC perturbation theorem. Furthermore, we reveal a distinctive anomalous valley Hall effect in which the valley Hall voltage is reversed within the same valley in ferrimagnet VCrSeTeO. This work proposes a strategy for realizing giant valley polarization and provides theoretical guidance for the application of ferrimagnetic ferrovalley semiconductors derived from altermagnets in valleytronics.

AI.tldr: This paper studies altermagnets and fully compensated ferrimagnets, revealing the mechanism for strain-driven transformation between them and proposing a new ferrimagnetic material (VCrSeTeO) with large valley polarization and anomalous valley Hall effect for valleytronics applications.
AI.motivation: The field lacks understanding of how to transform altermagnets to ferrimagnets and how to achieve large valley polarization. The researchers aimed to elucidate the strain-driven mechanism and design new materials combining advantages of both antiferromagnets and ferromagnets.
AI.method: The authors used theoretical calculations to study uniaxial strain-driven transformations from AM to FC-FIM, analyzed correlation between valley polarization and net magnetic moment, proposed VCrSeTeO monolayer, and investigated SOC effects using perturbation theorem.
AI.result: Valley polarization is positively correlated with net magnetic moment. VCrSeTeO achieves large intrinsic valley polarization (>400 meV under uniaxial strains with SOC). A distinctive anomalous valley Hall effect with reversed voltage within the same valley was revealed.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a strategy for achieving giant valley polarization and theoretical guidance for ferrimagnetic ferrovalley semiconductors in valleytronics applications.

title: Extension of the iterated perturbation theory at arbitrary fillings to nonequilibrium steady states
summary: We extend the Kajueter-Kotliar [Phys. Rev. Lett. 77, 131 (1996)] iterated perturbation theory (KK-IPT) away from half filling to nonequilibrium steady states. We benchmark the resulting nonequilibrium KK-IPT approach against the auxiliary master equation approach (AMEA), whose accuracy is controlled in and out of equilibrium. As expected, in equilibrium, KK-IPT reproduces the AMEA results for different fillings with high accuracy at the level of both spectral properties and electron densities. Out of equilibrium, we study quantum transport across a correlated impurity and compute the differential conductance and spectral functions. We find very good agreement between nonequilibrium KK-IPT and AMEA in the parameter regime where the latter is reliable, in particular at moderate temperatures and biases. These results support nonequilibrium KK-IPT as an approximate description of nonequilibrium steady states away from half filling. Although a controlled benchmark is not available in the low-temperature, low-bias regime, where AMEA becomes less reliable, nonequilibrium KK-IPT remains numerically stable in those regions, suggesting that it may provide a useful alternative for nonequilibrium calculations in this regime.

AI.tldr: This paper extends the Kajueter-Kotliar iterated perturbation theory (KK-IPT) from equilibrium half-filling to nonequilibrium steady states, benchmarks it against the auxiliary master equation approach (AMEA), and finds good agreement in both equilibrium and nonequilibrium regimes, especially at moderate temperatures and biases.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to extend an existing theoretical method (KK-IPT) from equilibrium half-filling to nonequilibrium steady states away from half-filling, and to validate its accuracy against a reliable benchmark method (AMEA).
AI.method: The methods include: (1) Extending KK-IPT away from half-filling to nonequilibrium steady states; (2) Benchmarking against AMEA; (3) Studying quantum transport across a correlated impurity; (4) Computing differential conductance and spectral functions.
AI.result: In equilibrium, KK-IPT reproduces AMEA results with high accuracy for different fillings. Out of equilibrium, good agreement between KK-IPT and AMEA at moderate temperatures and biases. KK-IPT remains numerically stable in low-temperature, low-bias regime where AMEA is less reliable.
AI.conclusion: Nonequilibrium KK-IPT provides a useful alternative for nonequilibrium calculations, especially in regimes where other methods become less reliable. It is numerically stable and accurate for moderate temperatures and biases.

title: Flat-band energy filtering in interacting systems: conditions for improving thermoelectric performances
summary: Motivated by recent theoretical and experimental studies on the role of flatbands in the thermoelectric properties of Ni$_3$In$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ compounds, we investigate electron transport in two minimal one-dimensional flatband models, the sawtooth and diamond chains, which differ in a crucial aspect: the flatband is separated from the dispersive band by a finite gap in the former, while the two bands touch in the latter. Using a non-equilibrium Green function framework with interactions treated at the Hartree-Fock and GW levels, we compute the full set of thermoelectric coefficients and the figure of merit $zT$ as functions of gate voltage and temperature. We show that, contrary to naive expectation, a perfectly isolated flat-band is a physically ill-founded thermoelectric: the electrical conductivity vanishes as the chemical potential enters the flat-band, rendering the large Seebeck coefficient and the apparent violation of the Wiedemann-Franz law physically meaningless. Optimal thermoelectric performance is instead achieved just below the flat-band edge, where the transmission function varies most rapidly with energy, consistent with the Mahan-Sofo picture, and requires a finite broadening of the flat-band through hybridization with dispersive states. We further show that electron-electron interactions renormalize the flat-band structure itself, inducing an interaction-driven narrowing of the bandwidth and, in the diamond chain, a correlation-induced opening of a gap between the flat-band and the dispersive band near half-filling. Mean-field treatments are found to systematically overestimate \(zT\), highlighting the importance of beyond-mean-field correlations for quantitatively reliable predictions in flat-band thermoelectrics.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates thermoelectric properties of 1D flatband models (sawtooth and diamond chains) using non-equilibrium Green function methods. It challenges the naive expectation that flatbands are good for thermoelectrics, showing that optimal performance occurs just below the flatband edge rather than at the flatband itself, and that electron-electron interactions significantly affect predictions.
AI.motivation: Motivated by theoretical and experimental studies on flatbands in Ni₃In₁₋ₓSnₓ thermoelectric compounds, the authors sought to understand the fundamental electron transport mechanisms in minimal 1D flatband models and resolve the apparent contradiction between expected high thermoelectric performance and practical observations.
AI.method: Non-equilibrium Green function framework with electron-electron interactions treated at two levels: Hartree-Fock (mean-field) and GW (beyond-mean-field) approximations. Computed full set of thermoelectric coefficients and figure of merit zT as functions of gate voltage and temperature.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Perfectly isolated flat-bands have vanishing electrical conductivity when chemical potential enters the flatband, making large Seebeck coefficients physically meaningless; (2) Optimal thermoelectric performance occurs just below the flatband edge where transmission varies most rapidly, consistent with Mahan-Sofo theory; (3) Electron-electron interactions renormalize flatband structure, narrowing bandwidth and opening correlation-induced gaps; (4) Mean-field treatments systematically overestimate zT values.
AI.conclusion: Flatband thermoelectrics require finite broadening through hybridization with dispersive states for optimal performance. Beyond-mean-field correlations are essential for quantitatively reliable predictions, as mean-field approaches significantly overestimate thermoelectric performance figures.

title: Spinon shift current in a noncentrosymmetric quantum spin chain
summary: We theoretically study direct current generation in a quantum spin chain induced by spinon excitations by light irradiation. We consider a s=1/2 1D antiferromagnetic XXZ model with magnetoelectric coupling that describes multiferroics with broken inversion symmetry. We perform the real-time simulation using infinite time-evolving block decimation (iTEBD), and demonstrate the direct current generation under light irradiation. By comparing the second order nonlinear conductivity and the two-spinon excitation spectra of 1D XXZ model, we confirm that the spinon excitations are the origin for the direct current generation in the quantum spin chain. We find that the bulk photovoltaic effect is driven by electric polarization carried by the spinons through the shift current mechanism, and thus is regarded as ``the spinon shift current''.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically investigates direct current generation in a quantum spin chain (s=1/2 1D XXZ antiferromagnet with magnetoelectric coupling) induced by spinon excitations through light irradiation, using iTEBD simulations. The research confirms that spinon excitations are the origin of DC generation via the shift current mechanism, termed "the spinon shift current."
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the mechanism of direct current generation in quantum spin chains induced by light irradiation, specifically to identify the role of spinon excitations in multiferroic materials with broken inversion symmetry.
AI.method: The method involves real-time simulation using infinite time-evolving block decimation (iTEBD), comparing second order nonlinear conductivity with two-spinon excitation spectra of the 1D XXZ model.
AI.result: The results confirm that spinon excitations drive the bulk photovoltaic effect through electric polarization carried by the spinons via the shift current mechanism, which the authors term "the spinon shift current."
AI.conclusion: The direct current generation in quantum spin chains under light irradiation originates from spinon excitations through the shift current mechanism, representing a new physical phenomenon called "the spinon shift current" in multiferroic materials.

title: Direct Orientation Contrast Imaging of Anti-Phase Domains on III-V Materials Using Scanning Electron Microscopy
summary: Direct orientation contrast imaging of zinc-blende III-V materials is studied using scanning electron microscopy. A quantitative approach is taken using a 3 μm thick orientation-patterned GaP grown on GaAs sample, studying the anti-phase domain contrast with respect to the electron beam energy and the tilt angle. A qualitative approach is taken for III-V grown on non-polar materials with and without chemical mechanical polishing. Finally, a processing of the acquired image for GaP on Si reveals in plane preferential anti-phase boundaries.

AI.tldr: This paper studies direct orientation contrast imaging of zinc-blende III-V materials using scanning electron microscopy, focusing on anti-phase domain contrast in orientation-patterned GaP grown on GaAs and Si substrates.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand and quantify anti-phase domain contrast in zinc-blende III-V semiconductor materials, which is crucial for characterizing crystal orientation and defects in semiconductor heterostructures used in optoelectronic and electronic devices.
AI.method: The study employs a quantitative approach using a 3 μm thick orientation-patterned GaP grown on GaAs sample, analyzing anti-phase domain contrast as a function of electron beam energy and tilt angle. Additionally, a qualitative approach is used for III-V materials grown on non-polar substrates with and without chemical mechanical polishing.
AI.result: The research reveals the relationship between anti-phase domain contrast and electron beam parameters (energy and tilt angle). For GaP on Si, image processing reveals in-plane preferential anti-phase boundaries, providing insights into the distribution and orientation of domain boundaries in these materials.
AI.conclusion: Direct orientation contrast imaging in SEM is an effective technique for characterizing orientation domains and anti-phase boundaries in zinc-blende III-V materials. The quantitative approach provides detailed insights into contrast mechanisms, while the qualitative studies on different substrates demonstrate the versatility of the method for various material systems.

title: Experimentally-validated multi-slice simulation of electron diffraction patterns
summary: High-Resolution Electron Backscatter Diffraction (HR-EBSD) has advanced rapidly in recent years, significantly improving elastic strain measurements and dislocation density evaluation with submicron spatial resolution. To achieve better accuracy in the measurements, high-quality dynamical simulation patterns are required to be matched with experimental ones. Currently, the most widely used pattern simulation method, the Bloch Wave method (BW), can accurately predict the positions and brightness of Kikuchi poles and bands, but is intrinsically limited to perfect crystal structures. Another simulation scheme, the multi-slice method (MS), follows the evolution of electron waves as they travel through the sample. MS is advantageous in simulating various defect structures with more diffraction details.  Yet, it is mainly considered for theoretical developments and has not been compared to experimental data.  This paper optimizes the MS method by abandoning the high-energy hypothesis and utilizing higher-order Taylor expansions to approach the forward-only Schrodinger equation. Experimental EBSD patterns of polycrystal Al-Mg alloys are used to challenge MS simulations as a reference for indexation.  It is demonstrated that the 5th-order expansion of MS, referred to as MS5, achieves a good balance between computational cost and pattern precision. A tailored isotropic distortion correction model and standard stereographic triangle reconstruction enhance the precision of MS5 to be comparable with BW. To the best of our knowledge, this study provides the first comparison of MS EBSD simulations with experimental data. It opens new possibilities for EBSD characterization, such as reproducing diffraction patterns of crystals with various defects.

AI.tldr: The paper optimizes the multi-slice (MS) electron microscopy simulation method for EBSD pattern simulation by using higher-order Taylor expansions, achieving accuracy comparable to the Bloch Wave method while being applicable to defective crystal structures.
AI.motivation: HR-EBSD needs high-quality dynamical simulation patterns for accurate elastic strain and dislocation measurements. The widely used Bloch Wave method is limited to perfect crystal structures, while the multi-slice method can handle defects but hasn't been validated against experimental data.
AI.method: The MS method was optimized by abandoning the high-energy hypothesis and using 5th-order Taylor expansions (MS5). Experimental EBSD patterns of polycrystal Al-Mg alloys were used for comparison. A tailored isotropic distortion correction model and standard stereographic triangle reconstruction were applied to enhance precision.
AI.result: MS5 achieves a good balance between computational cost and pattern precision, reaching precision comparable to the Bloch Wave method. This study provides the first comparison of MS EBSD simulations with experimental data.
AI.conclusion: This work opens new possibilities for EBSD characterization, enabling reproduction of diffraction patterns from crystals with various defects, significantly advancing the capability of EBSD for defective crystal analysis.

title: Identification and Structural Characterization of Twisted Atomically Thin Bilayer Materials by Deep Learning
summary: Two-dimensional materials are expected to play an important role in next-generation electronics and optoelectronic devices. Recently, twisted bilayer graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides have attracted significant attention due to their unique physical properties and potential applications. In this study we describe the use of optical microscopy to collect the color space of chemical vapor deposition (CVD) molybdenum disulfide ($\mbox{MoS}_2$), and the application of a semantic segmentation convolutional neural network (CNN) to accurately and rapidly identify thicknesses of $\mbox{MoS}_2$ flakes. A second CNN model is trained to provide precise predictions on the twist angle of CVD-grown bilayer flakes. This model harnessed a dataset comprising over 10,000 synthetic images, encompassing geometries spanning from hexagonal to triangular shapes. Subsequent validation of the deep learning predictions on twist angles was executed through the second harmonic generation and Raman spectroscopy. Our results introduce a scalable methodology for automated inspection of twisted atomically thin CVD-grown bilayer.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a deep learning-based approach using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for automated identification of MoS2 flake thicknesses and twist angles in CVD-grown bilayer materials, validated by second harmonic generation and Raman spectroscopy.
AI.motivation: Two-dimensional materials like twisted bilayer graphene and transition metal dichalcogenides are crucial for next-generation electronics and optoelectronic devices, but existing methods for characterizing CVD-grown bilayer MoS2 (thickness and twist angle) are time-consuming and not scalable. There is a need for automated, rapid identification methods.
AI.method: The study employed semantic segmentation CNN to identify MoS2 flake thicknesses from optical microscopy color space data, and a second CNN model trained on over 10,000 synthetic images (hexagonal to triangular shapes) to predict twist angles. Validation was performed using second harmonic generation and Raman spectroscopy.
AI.result: The semantic segmentation CNN accurately and rapidly identified thicknesses of MoS2 flakes from optical microscopy data. The second CNN model provided precise predictions on twist angles of CVD-grown bilayer flakes, validated through SHG and Raman spectroscopy.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a scalable deep learning-based methodology for automated inspection of CVD-grown twisted bilayer MoS2, enabling rapid identification of flake thicknesses and twist angles through optical microscopy combined with CNN models, validated by SHG and Raman spectroscopy.

title: Mircomechanical insights into unconstrained grain boundary sliding
summary: Grain boundary sliding (GBS) is a key deformation mechanism at high homologous temperatures in polycrystalline materials, however, its intrinsic behavior is often obscured by additional strain accommodation processes. In this study, dislocation-mediated unconstrained GBS was investigated using Ni bicrystal micropillars containing a single high-angle grain boundary. Micropillar compression tests were conducted over a temperature range from room temperature to $600\,^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and strain rates between $5\times10^{-4}$ and $10^{-1}\,\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. By comparing bicrystal and single-crystal responses, the intrinsic contribution of GBS was isolated. The strain-rate sensitivity remained low (SRS $\approx 0.034 \pm 0.017$), comparable to room temperature values, indicating the absence of diffusion-controlled accommodation mechanisms. The activation energy for GBS was determined to be $234\,\mathrm{kJ\,mol^{-1}}$, consistent with grain boundary diffusion-assisted glide of grain boundary dislocations. These results demonstrate that the high strain-rate sensitivity commonly associated with GBS in polycrystals originates primarily from accommodation processes rather than the intrinsic sliding mechanism.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates intrinsic grain boundary sliding (GBS) in Ni bicrystal micropillars at high temperatures, demonstrating that the sliding mechanism has low strain-rate sensitivity (SRS ≈ 0.034) and is controlled by grain boundary diffusion-assisted dislocation glide with an activation energy of 234 kJ/mol, rather than accommodation processes.
AI.motivation: Grain boundary sliding is a key deformation mechanism at high homologous temperatures, but its intrinsic behavior is often obscured by additional strain accommodation processes in polycrystalline materials, making it difficult to understand the true sliding mechanism.
AI.method: Ni bicrystal micropillars containing a single high-angle grain boundary were used to isolate intrinsic GBS. Micropillar compression tests were conducted at temperatures from room temperature to 600°C with strain rates between 5×10⁻⁴ and 10⁻¹ s⁻¹. The intrinsic GBS contribution was isolated by comparing bicrystal and single-crystal responses.
AI.result: The strain-rate sensitivity remained low (SRS ≈ 0.034 ± 0.017), comparable to room temperature values, indicating the absence of diffusion-controlled accommodation mechanisms. The activation energy for GBS was determined to be 234 kJ/mol, consistent with grain boundary diffusion-assisted glide of grain boundary dislocations.
AI.conclusion: The high strain-rate sensitivity commonly associated with GBS in polycrystals originates primarily from accommodation processes rather than the intrinsic sliding mechanism itself.

title: Formation of photoinduced space-charge field during in-bulk domain creation by femtosecond NIR laser irradiation in MgO:LN crystals
summary: We have studied the domain switching under NIR fs-laser irradiation in MgO:LN single crystals with special attention to the relative positions of the light-induced domains, microtracks and regions with modified refractive index in the vicinity of the focusing point. The optical imaging along X direction of the irradiated sample demonstrated the narrow microtracks and the lens-shaped regions ("lenses") located in the vicinity of the microtracks. The relative positions of light-induced microtracks, domains and lenses were revealed by overlapping of their images. We have found that the domain envelops the microtrack and partially intersects with the lens. The temperature stability of all light-induced objects during annealing was studied. It was shown that the local modification of the refractive index disappeared irreversibly while the microtrack and domain remain unchanged. The obtained local modification of the refractive index has been considered as a result of the photorefractive effect. The disappearance of the lens after annealing is caused by bulk screening of the photoinduced space-charge field by increased bulk conductivity. The generation of photovoltaic field by tightly focused NIR fs-laser irradiation close to the focusing point is reported for the first time to the authors' knowledge. It should be noted that in LN the photovoltaic field is codirectional with spontaneous polarization and thus cannot switch the polarization. However, it is possible in ferroelectrics with the opposite direction of the photovoltaic field and low value of threshold field. The revealed effect can be utilized for creation of 3D nonlinear photonic crystals by in-bulk domain engineering.

AI.tldr: This study investigates domain switching in MgO-doped lithium niobate (MgO:LN) crystals induced by NIR femtosecond laser irradiation, examining the spatial relationships between light-induced domains, microtracks, and refractive index-modified "lens" regions, along with their thermal stability.
AI.motivation: The motivation appears to be understanding the fundamental mechanisms of light-matter interaction in ferroelectric crystals, particularly how NIR fs-laser irradiation can induce domain switching and modified refractive index regions, with potential applications in creating 3D nonlinear photonic crystals through in-bulk domain engineering.
AI.method: The researchers used NIR fs-laser irradiation with tight focusing on MgO:LN single crystals, then employed optical imaging along the X direction to study the relative positions of microtracks, domains, and lens-shaped regions. They also studied thermal stability through annealing experiments.
AI.result: Key findings include: (1) Domain structures envelop microtracks and partially intersect with lens regions; (2) Refractive index modifications (lenses) disappear irreversibly upon annealing while microtracks and domains remain stable; (3) The photovoltaic field generation close to the focusing point is reported for the first time; (4) The lens formation is attributed to photorefractive effect, with disappearance caused by bulk screening of photoinduced space-charge fields.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that NIR fs-laser irradiation can generate photovoltaic fields in MgO:LN, creating stable domain structures and microtracks while producing temporary refractive index modifications. This effect shows promise for creating 3D nonlinear photonic crystals through in-bulk domain engineering, though the authors note the method works best in ferroelectrics with opposite photovoltaic field direction and low threshold field values.

title: Laser-written reconfigurable energy landscapes and programmable Moiré spin textures
summary: Magnetic textures are central to emerging spintronic and unconventional computing technologies due to their rich dynamics, topological properties and nanoscale dimensions. A major challenge remains achieving tunable, reversible, and spatially resolved control over these textures and their evolution as a function of external stimuli, by spatially reprogramming the magnetic energy landscape that governs their nucleation and stability. Here, we exploit a focused laser-assisted local field cooling technique that establishes a fast, non-contact and scalable platform for grayscale spin texture engineering.
