By non-destructively controlling the exchange-bias anisotropy with nanoscale resolution in thin-film heterostructures, this approach enables grayscale, reprogrammable control of the local energy profile, which we use to create magnetic patterns with highly controlled hysteresis, field-dependent readability and tunable switching thresholds. Leveraging this capability, we demonstrate information encoding with magnetic field-gated readability, and artificial spin metamaterials, stabilizing spin lattices with field-reconfigurable symmetries and creating artificial Moiré spin textures via the geometric superposition of twisted magnetic potentials. These results establish a versatile, reprogrammable platform that bridges the gap between application-oriented magnetic memory and fundamental studies of emergent order in artificial lattices.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a focused laser-assisted local field cooling technique for grayscale, reprogrammable control of magnetic textures in thin-film heterostructures. The method enables spatial control of exchange-bias anisotropy, allowing creation of magnetic patterns with tunable hysteresis, field-gated readability, artificial spin metamaterials, and artificial Moiré spin textures.
AI.motivation: The central challenge in spintronics and unconventional computing technologies is achieving tunable, reversible, and spatially resolved control over magnetic textures and their evolution through external stimuli, by spatially reprogramming the magnetic energy landscape.
AI.method: The authors exploit a focused laser-assisted local field cooling technique that establishes a fast, non-contact and scalable platform for grayscale spin texture engineering. This approach non-destructively controls the exchange-bias anisotropy with nanoscale resolution in thin-film heterostructures.
AI.result: The technique enables grayscale, reprogrammable control of the local energy profile, creating magnetic patterns with highly controlled hysteresis, field-dependent readability and tunable switching thresholds. Demonstrations include: (1) information encoding with magnetic field-gated readability, (2) artificial spin metamaterials stabilizing spin lattices with field-reconfigurable symmetries, and (3) artificial Moiré spin textures via geometric superposition of twisted magnetic potentials.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a versatile, reprogrammable platform that bridges the gap between application-oriented magnetic memory and fundamental studies of emergent order in artificial lattices.

title: ChemGraph-XANES: An Agentic Framework for XANES Simulation and Analysis
summary: Computational X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) is widely used to probe local coordination environments, oxidation states, and electronic structure in chemically complex systems. However, the use of computational XANES at scale is constrained more by workflow complexity than by the underlying simulation method itself. To address this challenge, we present ChemGraph-XANES, an agentic framework for automated XANES simulation and analysis that unifies natural-language task specification, structure acquisition, FDMNES input generation, task-parallel execution, spectral normalization, and provenance-aware data curation. Built on ASE, FDMNES, Parsl, and a LangGraph/LangChain-based tool interface, the framework exposes XANES workflow operations as typed Python tools that can be orchestrated by large language model (LLM) agents. In multi-agent mode, a retrieval-augmented expert agent consults the FDMNES manual to ground parameter selection, while executor agents translate user requests into structured tool calls. We demonstrate documentation-grounded parameter retrieval and show that the same workflow supports both explicit structure-file inputs and chemistry-level natural-language requests. Because independent XANES calculations are naturally task-parallel, the framework is well suited for high-throughput deployment on high-performance computing (HPC) systems, enabling scalable XANES database generation for downstream analysis and machine-learning applications. ChemGraph-XANES thus provides a reproducible and extensible workflow layer for physics-based XANES simulation, spectral curation, and agent-compatible computational spectroscopy.

AI.tldr: ChemGraph-XANES is an agentic framework that automates computational X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) simulations by unifying natural-language task specification, structure acquisition, FDMNES input generation, task-parallel execution, spectral normalization, and provenance-aware data curation using ASE, FDMNES, Parsl, and LangGraph/LangChain.
AI.motivation: Computational XANES is widely used to probe local coordination environments, oxidation states, and electronic structure, but its use at scale is constrained by workflow complexity rather than the underlying simulation method itself.
AI.method: The framework builds on ASE, FDMNES, Parsl, and a LangGraph/LangChain-based tool interface to expose XANES workflow operations as typed Python tools that can be orchestrated by LLM agents. In multi-agent mode, a retrieval-augmented expert agent consults the FDMNES manual to ground parameter selection, while executor agents translate user requests into structured tool calls.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates documentation-grounded parameter retrieval and supports both explicit structure-file inputs and chemistry-level natural-language requests. It is well suited for high-throughput deployment on HPC systems for scalable XANES database generation.
AI.conclusion: ChemGraph-XANES provides a reproducible and extensible workflow layer for physics-based XANES simulation, spectral curation, and agent-compatible computational spectroscopy.

title: Bridging Atomistic and Continuum Descriptions of Nanoscale Dislocation Loops in Tungsten
summary: In order to predict the long-term effects of irradiation on the material properties of tungsten, a continuum approach to simulating the interactions of dislocation loops, which arise from radiation damage, is proposed. Continuum models of the displacement, strain and stress fields produced by dislocation loops exhibit unphysical singularities near the defect core, but are thought to accurately capture atomistic displacements in the far-field. A linear elastic model of nanoscale dislocation loops in tungsten is developed, and the model is verified using atomistic simulations to ensure that the model is informed by lower-length scale phenomena such that the physics of the problem is correctly captured. We discuss the model and its advantages, and show that predictions produced by atomistic simulations do indeed agree well with the far-field behaviour of the continuum model when dislocation loops are far from material boundaries. In particular, we robustly demonstrate that the decay rate of atomistic results and continuum results coincide with one another, and show that the results converge as the size of the atomistic simulations approach the far-field limit.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a linear elastic continuum model for simulating nanoscale dislocation loops in tungsten under radiation damage, verified against atomistic simulations. The results demonstrate good agreement in far-field behavior between both approaches, with converging decay rates validating the continuum method for predicting radiation effects on tungsten properties.
AI.motivation: To predict long-term effects of irradiation on material properties of tungsten by developing a continuum approach to simulating dislocation loops arising from radiation damage, while addressing the unphysical singularities in traditional continuum models near defect cores.
AI.method: A linear elastic continuum model of nanoscale dislocation loops in tungsten was developed and verified using atomistic simulations to ensure the model captures lower-length scale physics correctly.
AI.result: Atomistic simulation predictions agree well with the far-field behavior of the continuum model. The decay rate of atomistic and continuum results coincide, and results converge as atomistic simulation size approaches the far-field limit.
AI.conclusion: The continuum model accurately captures far-field behavior when dislocation loops are far from material boundaries, with atomistic and continuum results showing converging decay rates as simulation size increases, validating the continuum approach for modeling radiation damage in tungsten.

title: Atomistic Mechanisms of Stress-Dependent Molten Salt Corrosion in NiCr Alloys
summary: Ni-based structural alloys in molten salt environments often experience simultaneous mechanical loading and corrosive attack, yet the mechanisms governing stress-corrosion interactions remain unclear. Prior studies largely emphasize tensile stress, while the role of compressive stress has received limited attention. Here, reactive molecular dynamics simulations are used to investigate the coupled effects of applied strain and corrosion in Ni$_{0.75}$Cr$_{0.25}$ exposed to molten FLiNaK at 800$^\circ$C. A $\Sigma5(210)$ grain boundary model is subjected to tensile (+4%) to compressive (-4%) uniaxial strains, and corrosion behavior is evaluated through fluorine adsorption, charge redistribution, and grain boundary evolution. Tensile strain accelerates intergranular corrosion by reducing local atomic packing through elastic dilation and increasing excess free volume at the grain boundary, which enhances atomic mobility and salt infiltration. In contrast, compressive strain suppresses corrosion by promoting the formation of a ridge-like surface layer along the grain boundary, limiting salt access to the underlying alloy. These results provide atomistic insight into how stress states influence grain boundary corrosion in molten salts.

AI.tldr: This paper uses reactive molecular dynamics to investigate stress-corrosion interactions in Ni0.75Cr0.25 alloy in molten FLiNaK at 800°C. The study reveals that tensile strain accelerates intergranular corrosion by increasing free volume and atomic mobility, while compressive strain suppresses corrosion by forming a protective ridge-like surface layer at grain boundaries.
AI.motivation: Prior studies on stress-corrosion interactions largely emphasize tensile stress, while the role of compressive stress has received limited attention. This work addresses this gap by investigating how compressive vs tensile stress affects corrosion mechanisms in Ni-based alloys in molten salt environments.
AI.method: Reactive molecular dynamics simulations were used to investigate coupled effects of applied strain (tensile +4% to compressive -4%) and corrosion on Ni0.75Cr0.25 alloy with Σ5(210) grain boundary exposed to molten FLiNaK at 800°C. Corrosion behavior was evaluated through fluorine adsorption, charge redistribution, and grain boundary evolution.
AI.result: Tensile strain accelerates intergranular corrosion by reducing local atomic packing through elastic dilation and increasing excess free volume at the grain boundary, which enhances atomic mobility and salt infiltration. In contrast, compressive strain suppresses corrosion by promoting the formation of a ridge-like surface layer along the grain boundary, limiting salt access to the underlying alloy.
AI.conclusion: This study provides atomistic-level insight into how stress states (tensile vs compressive) influence grain boundary corrosion in molten FLiNaK salt. Tensile strain promotes corrosion while compressive strain suppresses it through protective layer formation.
